comments.text: In [Er61] the problem is only stated for Schur numbers. Does anyone know the earliest place in which it is stated for Ramsey?
I don't know, but would like to. The fact that this is a problem of Erdős, and the two prizes listed, I took from Chung's site, which gives no further reference.

Here's a cute related problem that people might be interested in. Let $\chi(n)$ denote the smallest number of sum-free sets that partition $\mathbb{F}_2^n\setminus \{0\}$. Via a subtle recursion combined with the Schur argument, we (with Anurag Bishnoi and Ananth Ravi) have recently (well, the project started 2 years ago) proved that for every integer $n\ge 2$, $$L:=\lim_{k \rightarrow \infty} R(3;k)^{1/k} \ge 2^{(n-1)/(\chi(n)-1)}.$$ So it's worth trying to find good upper bounds on $\chi(n)$ for small values of $n$. For example, $\chi(8)\le 5$ or $\chi(13)\le 8$ (if true) would already improve the lower bound on $L$ to $2^{7/4} \approx 3.364$ or $2^{12/7}\approx 3.2813$, respectively. Currently, the values of $\chi(n)$ are known for all $n\leq 7$. Asymptotically, $\frac{(1-o(1)) n}{ \log_2(n)} \le \chi(n)\leq \lfloor \frac{2n}{3} \rfloor +1$.

The mentioned base of the lower bound $(321)^{1/5}=3.1717\ldots$ of Exoo (1994) was improved to $(1073)^{1/6} =3.1996\ldots$ by Fredericksen and Sweet (2000) doi:10.37236/1510 via an estimate of the sixth Schur number; compare the discussion in doi:10.37236/1788. The current best lower bound I can find is in this (unpublished) preprint of Ageron et al (2021) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2112.03175, where they claim $\lim_{k \rightarrow \infty} R(3;k)^{1/k} \geq (380)^{1/5} \approx 3.2806\ldots$ (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
